## Top pick rationale
The **Template Method** for power-free words (A2) is the strongest structural analog because it specifically addresses the "consecutive" constraint, which is the defining difficulty of Erdős 938. In combinatorics on words, a "square" or "k-power" is a repetition of a pattern in consecutive positions of a sequence; similarly, Erdos 938 asks if the local pattern of an arithmetic progression occurs infinitely in the consecutive sequence of powerful numbers. While number-theoretic methods (like Arakelov theory) are superior for proving finiteness of solutions to equations like $n, n+1, n+2$, they are blind to the "consecutive" property ($n_k, n_{k+1}, n_{k+2}$). The Template Method, combined with the recent "sieve technique" for abelian power-free morphisms (Eyidoğan et al., 2026), provides a formal framework for verifying that certain local patterns cannot persist or repeat in sequences with specific growth and structural constraints. This shift from Diophantine analysis to pattern-avoidance in symbolic dynamics offers the most direct path to modeling the "consecutive" aspect of the problem.

## Top pick rationale

A1 is the cleanest analogy because it is not just metaphorical: erdos_938 is exactly the statement that the powerful-number gap word has only finitely many adjacent repeated letters. That suggests a concrete first action: study the gap sequence directly and ask whether existing repetition-avoidance tools have any deterministic analogue for arithmetic gap words. The likely obstruction is also clear: Thue-style methods construct words, while here the word is imposed by the powerful numbers. Still, this framing separates the additive part from the multiplicative part better than the raw AP formulation does. A2 and A3 are closer to the visible arithmetic-progression equation, but they miss the “consecutive terms” feature that seems structurally central.
